1:
On Matthew 17:1 — “And after six days Jesus taketh Peter, James, and John his brother, and bringeth them up into an high mountain apart” — the cloud of witnesses opens the Transfiguration, fixing its timing, weighing the choice of three, and reading the ascent.

The grammatical-historical commentators are busiest with the chronology and circumstances. With one voice they reconcile Matthew’s and Mark’s “after six days” with Luke’s “about eight days” (Luke 9:28) as a difference of reckoning — Matthew counts only the intervening days, Luke adds the first and last (Barnes, Ellicott, Clarke), while Robertson takes “about eight days” simply to mark a week. The three, they note, form an inner circle, “the elect among the elect” (Ellicott), present also at the raising of Jairus’s daughter and in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:37).

The Latin fathers read the scene typologically. The “six days,” says Raban, answer to the six ages of the world after which the resurrection comes; the three taken up while the rest remain below figure that “many are called, but few chosen” (Remigius, Raban). Remigius bids all who would contemplate God rise, with the disciples, above the low pleasures of the present world, and finds in the brightness of the Lord’s face “some illustration of the future resurrection,” when the righteous too shall shine as the sun. Hilary sees Moses and Elias as the Law and the Prophets, between whom Christ stands in his kingdom; and Leo, in his great sermon, sets the glory against the passion just foretold, so that faith might be fortified.

The Antiochene Chrysostom asks why the vision waited: to spare the other disciples any pang of jealousy, and to kindle in the chosen three a keener desire. The Reformed voices weigh the purpose — Calvin rejects the common notion that it was merely to steel these three against the scandal of the cross (for then why deprive the rest?), finding rather a confirmation of Christ’s heavenly glory; Gill, with the Puritan Henry, hears in it the “power and coming” that Peter later recalls (2 Peter 1:16), an earnest of the kingdom. Where the witnesses divide is the site: Wesley and an old tradition name Mount Tabor, while Robertson and the moderns prefer the high slopes of Hermon. The Alexandrian Origen stands apart with a mystical reading: the Word “has various forms, appearing to each man according as He knows that it will be expedient,” and only he who has passed the six days above the things of this world beholds Jesus transfigured before the eyes of the heart.
2:
On Matthew 17:2 — “And was transfigured before them: and his face did shine as the sun, and his raiment was white as the light” — the witnesses agree, almost to a voice, that the change was of appearance, not of substance: the indwelling Godhead shone out through the veil of the flesh.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the word. To “transfigure,” says Barnes, is to change the appearance or form, not the substance — here the face shone “as the sun,” as Moses’ face once shone (Exodus 34:29). Robertson notes the verb (metemorphothe) marks the essence or inner form (morphe) as against the outward fashion (schema), the same contrast Paul draws in Romans 12:2 and 1 Corinthians 7:31; Ellicott observes the word is elsewhere rendered “transformed,” and adds Luke’s profound note (Luke 9:29) that the change came while Christ was praying. Clarke records the variant “white as snow” (Mark 9:3), and Lightfoot reads the scene as a sealing, as at the baptism, of Christ for his high-priestly office.

The Reformed voices read it doctrinally. Gill says neither the substance nor the shape of the body was altered; rather the form of God showed itself visibly through the form of a servant (Philippians 2:7). Calvin, marking that it happened in the act of prayer, denies that Christ needed to ask for what he possessed; throughout his humiliation he ascribed all to the Father and prayed to teach us by example. Wesley, with the Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke, puts it warmly: “the indwelling Deity darted out its rays through the veil of the flesh.”

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem turns the verse apologetically: this shining one is He who was seen of Moses, the spiritual Rock that followed Israel (1 Corinthians 10:4); the prophets beheld Christ, “as far as each was able.” The Alexandrian Origen alone reaches for mystery, asking whether to those below Christ kept the form of a servant while to those who climbed the mount he wore the form of God — a thing, he warns, to be heard “spiritually.” But no school dissents from the common confession: the glory was always his; the Transfiguration only drew back the veil.
3:
On Matthew 17:3 — “And, behold, there appeared unto them Moses and Elias talking with him” — the witnesses dwell on the fitness of the two figures, the manner of their presence, and the witness they bear.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the pair as Law and Prophets doing homage to the Gospel. Moses, says Barnes, gave the Law whose types now find fulfilment; Elijah, the great prophet, was taken to heaven without seeing death (2 Kings 2:11). Robertson notes that “both had mystery connected with their deaths,” that the Jewish apocalypses fed a popular expectation of their return, that one represented law and the other prophecy while Jesus represented grace, and that “sufficient objectivity is guaranteed by the vision being enjoyed by all three” (Bruce); they spoke, Luke records, of his decease — the cross (Luke 9:31). Ellicott finds “a singular fitness in each case,” the identification of the two intuitive, granted by a spiritual illumination. Clarke supposes Elijah came in his translated body, and Moses’ body raised as a pledge of the resurrection, for Christ comes to judge quick and dead and “we shall not all die, but all shall be changed” (1 Corinthians 15:51).

The Reformed voices take up the old question whether the two were actually present or only shown in vision. Calvin grants the subject “admits of arguments on both sides,” yet judges it “more probable that they were actually brought to that place” — “there is no absurdity in this supposition; for God has bodies and souls in his hand, and can restore the dead to life at his pleasure” — though they did not rise of their own accord. Gill simply marks that they appeared to the disciples.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley gathers the scene into one chorus of testimony: “Moses, the giver of the law, Elijah, the most zealous of all the prophets, and God speaking from heaven, all bore witness to him” — the full confirmation of the disciples’ faith. So the schools converge: the Law and the Prophets attend their Lord, and point past themselves to him.
4:
On Matthew 17:4 — “Then answered Peter… Lord, it is good for us to be here: let us make here three tabernacles” — the witnesses are nearly unanimous that Peter’s proposal, however well-meant, was spoken without understanding.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. A tabernacle, Barnes explains, is a tent — a temporary shelter, not a permanent dwelling; Peter, rejoiced at the vision, wished to prolong it, and Mark adds “they knew not what they said” (Mark 9:6; Luke 9:33). Robertson is blunt — “Peter to the front again, but not greatly to his credit” — yet notes he “makes a definite proposal,” and that, as the three were “heavy with sleep” (Luke 9:32), it is “not well to take Peter too seriously on this occasion.” Ellicott traces the “naïveté” of the words and notes Mark’s blunter “Rabbi”; Lightfoot reconstructs the night: the form of his face and garments changed while he prayed, Moses and Elias discoursing of his death while the disciples were “overcharged with sleep,” and on waking — “O what a spectacle had they!” — Peter would detain the prophets now departing.

The Reformed voices read his heart. Calvin says Peter feared the delightful exhibition would vanish at the prophets’ departure, and was “captivated by the loveliness of what he beheld,” as in the psalm, “In thy presence is fulness of joy” (Psalm 16:11); yet his desire was foolish — first, because he did not comprehend the design of the vision, and second, because he would have fixed as permanent what was given only for a season. The Wesleyan-Arminian voices agree: Wesley calls them “the words of rapturous surprise” — three tents, not six, “because the apostles desired to be with their Master” — while Clarke thinks Peter, still expecting a temporal kingdom, wished to keep Moses and Elijah at his Lord’s side.

The Alexandrian Origen alone presses the puzzle theologically, asking whether Peter spoke “as in a trance, being filled with the spirit which moved him” — though, he is careful to add, not the Holy Spirit, since both Mark and Luke testify that he “wist not what to answer.” The schools meet in one verdict: a sincere rapture, but an uncomprehending one.
5:
On Matthew 17:5 — the bright cloud, and the voice, “This is my beloved Son… hear ye him” — the witnesses are agreed: the cloud is the token of the divine Presence, the voice is the Father’s testimony, and the command “hear him” sets Christ above the Law and the Prophets.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the cloud as the Shechinah, the symbol of God’s presence that led Israel (Exodus 24:15–17) and filled the Temple (1 Kings 8:10; Ellicott adds Exodus 33:9). Clarke prefers the reading “a cloud of light” and would render “surrounded” rather than “overshadowed.” They mark that the words echo the baptism (Matthew 3:17); but, says Robertson, where they were there spoken to Jesus, here the voice speaks about him to the three — and “hear ye him,” even when he speaks of his death, is “a sharp rebuke to Peter.” Lightfoot hears the voice sealing him the true Messiah (2 Peter 1:17).

The Latin fathers read “hear ye him” as the supersession of the old covenant: let “the shadow of the Law be past, and the types of the Prophets,” and follow the one light of the Gospel (Remigius), who is the Prophet Moses foretold (Deuteronomy 18:18). Jerome notes the Father answers for the Son — “He that sent me, he beareth witness of me” (John 5:37); and the Gloss finds the whole Trinity manifested, as at the baptism. The Antiochene Chrysostom marks that the cloud is bright, not terrifying, because here Christ would teach, not affright; and the Father, “greater than all,” speaks so the disciples may know the voice is from God.

The Reformed Calvin reads the overshadowing as mercy in restraint: their eyes were covered to teach them they were “not yet prepared for beholding the brightness of the heavenly glory,” a lesson in humility against prying into hidden things. Wesley sums the command: hear him “as superior even to Moses and the prophets” (Deuteronomy 18:15); and the Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem draws the worship home — the Father accepts no service that does not honour the Son. Origen alone ventures that the bright cloud may be the Father’s power, or the Spirit, or the Saviour himself overshadowing Law, Prophets, and Gospel together.
6:
On Matthew 17:6 — “And when the disciples heard it, they fell on their face, and were sore afraid” — the witnesses agree that the terror was the natural effect of the divine Presence, and was permitted for the disciples’ good.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe the cause. Barnes: they entered the cloud (Luke 9:34) and were awed at the presence of God, for “no man can see God and live,” and only the glory of God shining in the face of Christ can be borne (2 Corinthians 4:6). Ellicott sets their dread beside Israel’s at the shining face of Moses (Exodus 34:30) and the priests’ who could not stand to minister for the cloud (1 Kings 8:11): they “lie prostrate on the ground in speechless terror… They have seen the glory of the Lord: can they hear His voice and live?” Clarke gathers the parallels — “dismayed by the voice, and dazzled by the glory” — of Daniel (Daniel 8:17) and Saul of Tarsus (Acts 9:4), who likewise fell.

The Reformed Calvin finds a purpose in the fear: “God intended that the disciples should be struck with this terror, in order to impress more fully on their hearts the remembrance of the vision.” Yet he marks the weakness of our nature, which trembles at the voice of God: “if ungodly men mock at God, or despise him without concern, it is because God does not address them so as to cause his presence to be felt; but the majesty of God, as soon as we perceive him, must unavoidably cast us down.”

The Alexandrian Origen reads the prostration as the inability of even these three to bear “the glory of the voice and power resting upon it”: having understood that the Son of God had been holding conference with Moses and Elias, they “fell on their face” and besought God, “sore afraid at the supernatural sight.” The witnesses thus speak as one: holy fear is the right response of mortal flesh to the unveiled presence of God.

Gill adds that the disciples “apprehended” the voice “came from God, and was uttered with so much majesty” — so their falling was the fit and inevitable answer of flesh to the unveiled holiness of God.
7:
On Matthew 17:7 — “And Jesus came and touched them, and said, Arise, and be not afraid” — the witnesses dwell on the tenderness of the act and on Christ’s mediating office.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the gentleness. Robertson notes simply, “tenderness in their time of fear”; Ellicott calls act and word both expressive of “an almost brotherly tenderness” — the touch of the hand they had so often grasped (Matthew 14:31) and the familiar words that had stilled their fear in the storm (Matthew 14:27) recall them “again to the realities of life.” Clarke, thinking the scene was by night, sets it beside Daniel, who in a deep sleep was touched and “set upright” (Daniel 8:18), and draws the lesson that “from Jesus alone are we to expect divine communications, and by his power only are we able to bear and improve them”; Barnes adds the parallels of Daniel 10:10 and Revelation 1:17.

The Reformed voices read the office. Gill says the disciples lay at some distance, and Christ, though clothed with glory, “did not disdain” to come to their relief, but “acts the part of a mediator between God and them,” laying hold to raise those whom the majesty of God’s voice had cast down — the Persic version, he notes, reads that “he… brought them to themselves,” for they were ready to faint. Calvin presses the same: Christ raises the fallen and so “performs his office; for he came down to us for this very purpose, that by his guidance believers might boldly enter into the presence of God,” that the majesty which would otherwise “swallow up all flesh” might no longer terrify them; and he comforts not by words only but encourages them.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley adds that “doubtless the same moment he gave them courage and strength.” The witnesses are one: the hand that touches is the Mediator’s, who alone makes the presence of God bearable to fallen men.

Calvin marks that the comfort came not by word alone: “nor is it only by his words that he comforts, but… he encourages them” — the touch itself a pledge that the Mediator stands between them and the consuming majesty.
8:
On Matthew 17:8 — “And when they had lifted up their eyes, they saw no man, save Jesus only” — the witnesses agree that the vanishing of Moses and Elijah leaves Christ alone in view, and that this is the very point of the scene.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the contrast. Robertson notes that, after the reassuring touch, they lifted their eyes and “Moses and Elijah were gone in the bright cloud” — Jesus only. Ellicott hears in “Jesus only,” following the “Be not afraid,” a marked rebuke to Peter’s rash wish for three tabernacles: it was not “good” for frail men to tarry long “in the immediate glory of the Presence,” and it was a relief to see Jesus as they had been wont to see him. “Moments of spiritual ecstasy are few and far between,” he adds, “and it is good for us that it should be so,” that we may carry “the fragrance and power of their memory into the work of our common life.” He even turns apologist for the narrative: the rationalistic method is “altogether inapplicable here,” the mythical theory has nothing in Jewish messianic expectation to suggest such a legend, and the allusive references of John 1:14 and 2 Peter 1:16 carry “evidential weight.”

The Reformed voices make it doctrine. Calvin: that in the end they saw Christ alone means “the Law and the Prophets had a temporary glory, that Christ alone might remain fully in view.” If we would profit by Moses, “we must not stop with him, but must endeavour to be conducted by his hand to Christ,” of whom he and all the rest are ministers; and the verse condemns those who confound Christ “with saints of the lowest rank.” The mistake’s origin he sees in the disciples themselves: “so long as they were terrified by the majesty of God, their minds wandered in search of men,” but when Christ gently raised them up, “they saw him” alone. Gill marks that they “looked round about,” as Mark says, to see whether the figures and the cloud remained. So the schools converge on the single lesson of the vanishing: when the witnesses withdraw, the Word remains, and he alone is to be heard and seen.
9:
On Matthew 17:9 — “Tell the vision to no man, until the Son of man be risen again from the dead” — the witnesses ask why the glory must be kept secret, and answer with one voice: its time had not yet come.

The grammatical-historical commentators give the reasons. Barnes: the vision was designed to confirm that Jesus is the Messiah, and would be needed as evidence after the crucifixion — three witnesses, as the law required — whereas telling it now would provoke the Jews and endanger his life; “his time was not yet come.” Robertson notes the construction (“until” with the subjunctive) points to the future resurrection, which Jesus hoped “might stand them in good stead at his death.” Clarke holds the whole emblematic scene was meant to prove “the reality of the world of spirits,” the immortality of the soul, and the resurrection of the body, and so must be kept secret till his death and resurrection had “accomplished vision and prophecy,” lest it irritate the Jewish rulers; Ellicott observes the prohibition included even the other apostles, who in their lower stage would have distrusted or perverted a second-hand report.

The Reformed Calvin agrees the time was not come: the disciples themselves would not have believed it without “a more striking proof of his glory in his resurrection”; only after his divine power was openly displayed could the vision be admitted, proving that even in his self-emptying (Philippians 2:7) he “continued to retain his divinity entire, though it was concealed under the veil of the flesh.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley adds a pastoral motive: not to the rest of the disciples, lest they be “grieved and discouraged” at being left out, nor to others, lest it enrage some and be disbelieved by others — “till the resurrection should make it credible.”

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem, looking ahead to that rising, meets the scoffer who doubts a three-days’ resurrection by appealing to the Lord’s own sign: “as Jonas was three days and three nights in the whale’s belly; so shall the Son of man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth” (Matthew 12:40).
10:
On Matthew 17:10 — “Why then say the scribes that Elias must first come?” — the witnesses trace the disciples’ puzzle to a scribal tradition drawn from Malachi, and begin to untangle it.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the question in its rabbinic context. Barnes: now satisfied that Jesus is the Christ, the disciples recall the common doctrine that Elijah should appear before the Messiah — hence the “then.” Robertson notes the scribes built on Malachi 4:5; Ellicott judges the question came from the three, brooding over Elijah’s sudden appearance and yet more sudden disappearance on the mount. Lightfoot heaps up the Jewish passages — “it would be an infinite task to produce all” — citing Kimchi on Malachi, that “God shall restore the soul of Elias, which ascended.” Clarke marks that, having just seen Elijah return to heaven, they were perplexed by the tradition founded on Malachi 4:5–6.

The Antiochene Chrysostom presses the scribes’ fault: the Scriptures “speak of two comings of Christ; that which has taken place, and that which is yet to be,” but the scribes, “blinding the people,” spoke only of the second, and so misled them about Elijah too — whence the current expectation reflected in the Samaritan woman (John 4:25) and the question put to the Baptist, “Art thou Elias, or the Prophet?” (John 1:21).

The Latin fathers read with their accustomed reach: Jerome says the disciples took the mountain-vision for “His coming in glory”; Augustine glosses “restore all things” of those whom Antichrist’s persecution shall overthrow; Hilary and Raban see John foreshadowed under the name of Elias. The Alexandrian Origen, alone, turns aside to forbid the doctrine of transmigration: John came “in the spirit and power of Elias,” not in Elijah’s soul. Calvin adds that the disciples wrongly imagined the resurrection to be the immediate commencement of the kingdom, while Wesley draws a missionary corollary — if he is come, “should we not rather tell every man?”

Origen adds that the disciples “who went up with Jesus remembered the traditions of the scribes concerning Elijah” — so the question rose not from idle curiosity but from a real collision between what they had been taught and what they had just seen on the mount.
11:
On Matthew 17:11 — “Elias truly shall first come, and restore all things” — the witnesses agree that Christ concedes the scribes’ doctrine to be true in substance, while correcting how they read it.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the word “restore.” Barnes: it means to put into a former situation — to heal, correct, set in proper order (cf. Matthew 12:13) — and Christ affirms that the scribes taught truly that Elijah comes before the Messiah. Clarke notes the Septuagint rendering Christ echoes, “he will turn the heart of the fathers to the children” (Malachi 4:6). Lightfoot recites the Jewish legends of what Elijah would restore — he would “purify the bastards… render to Israel the pot of manna, the vial of holy oil, the vial of water; and there are some who say, the rod of Aaron” — but corrects their drift: “He shall restore… not into the former state, but into a better,” the “times of restitution” framing all things into a gospel-state (Acts 3:21).

The Reformed Calvin states the resolution plainly: “every thing which Malachi uttered was true, but… his prediction had been misunderstood and distorted.” John, who came to restore the fallen condition of the Church, was given by Malachi the name of Elijah (Malachi 4:5), and the scribes “rashly interpreted” this as if the Tishbite (1 Kings 17:1) were to return a second time to the world; Gill marks Christ speaks here “by way of concession.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley sums the office: Elijah was to “regulate all things, in order to the coming of Christ.” Only Ellicott keeps the words deliberately open: they are “enigmatic,” and have been taken by many to promise that “Elijah shall come in person before the yet future… great second Advent,” so that the prophecy might “yet have a literal fulfilment” — even though John, who confessed he was not Elijah, has already fulfilled it in part.

So the schools converge: Malachi’s word stands, fulfilled already in the Baptist who came to set the people in order; only Ellicott keeps a door ajar for a further, literal coming of Elijah before the end.
12:
On Matthew 17:12 — “But I say unto you, That Elias is come already, and they knew him not… Likewise shall also the Son of man suffer of them” — the witnesses agree that the promised Elijah is John the Baptist, fulfilled in spirit, not in person.

The grammatical-historical commentators make the identification precise. Barnes: John came “in the spirit and power of Elias” (Luke 1:17); the old word “listed” means “to choose, to desire” (John 3:8), so they “did to him as they pleased,” putting him to death (Matthew 14:10); and Mark adds that the Son of man too “must suffer many things, and be set at nought.” Robertson: Jesus identifies John with Malachi’s promise, “though not the real Elijah in person, which John denied” (John 1:21); “they knew him not” (epiginosko, to recognise) means “just as they do not know Jesus now… They killed John as they will Jesus.” Ellicott reads the words as the emphatic repetition of Matthew 11:14, “decisive” that the prophecy was fulfilled in the Baptist, who need not look for “any other personal appearance”; Clarke narrows “knew him not” to the rulers, who “have not acknowledged him.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reconciles the words with John’s own denial: “I am not Elijah” (John 1:21) meant “I am not Elijah the Tishbite, come again into the world” — yet he was “the person of whom Malachi prophesied under that name.”

The Alexandrian Origen lingers, as on the previous verses, to bar the doctrine of transmigration: it is not that one soul was “the same in Elijah and in John,” for the angel’s word to Zacharias — that Elisabeth should bear him a son (Luke 1:13) — shows John was begotten in the ordinary way, not by a returning soul. So the witnesses agree in substance: John is the Elijah Malachi foretold, coming in his spirit and meeting a like rejection that prefigures the Son of man’s own suffering.

The witnesses thus read a double rejection foretold: as they “knew not” the Elijah sent before, so they will not know the Son of man who follows — and the one martyrdom is made the shadow of the other.
13:
On Matthew 17:13 — “Then the disciples understood that he spake unto them of John the Baptist” — the witnesses, few and brief here, mark the moment the disciples finally grasp the spiritual sense of the prophecy they had questioned.

The grammatical-historical commentators note both the clarity and the slowness. Robertson, marking the verb as “one of the three k aorists,” says “it was plain enough even for them. John was Elijah in spirit and had prepared the way for the Messiah.” Clarke explains how they came to it: “when he spoke of the sufferings of this prophetic Elijah, and also of his own, which had been the subject of the conversation on the mount, during the transfiguration, they clearly apprehended that he spoke of John the Baptist.” Ellicott finds the verse “suggestive both as indicating the conclusion in which they ultimately rested, and the frankness with which they owned how slowly they had passed from the literalism of the scribes to a true apprehension of the spiritual meaning of the prophecy in question.” Barnes simply refers the reader back to the question of v.10.

The Reformed Gill agrees: “by his saying that Elias was come, and by the account he gave of his ill usage, it was clear to them” of whom he spoke.

The witnesses leave little independent comment on this closing verse, resting in the conclusion already drawn at vv.11–12; their shared note is a quiet one. Understanding came not by a fresh revelation but by submitting their inherited, scribal expectation of a literal Elijah to the plain word of Christ — a pattern, as Ellicott implies, of how the literal slowly yields to the spiritual, and a frank acknowledgment, recorded by the Evangelist, of how long even the chosen three were in coming to it.

Mark records the same recognition more briefly, the disciples drawing it from the Lord’s own word that “Elias is indeed come, and they have done unto him whatsoever they listed” (Mark 9:13). The witnesses add little beyond this here; their shared and modest point is that the meaning, once hidden behind the scribes’ literalism, now lay open — not by new sight, but by trusting the plain saying of Christ.
14:
On Matthew 17:14 — “And when they were come to the multitude, there came to him a certain man, kneeling down to him” — the witnesses read the descent from the mount of glory to the crowd of need, and the father’s troubled appeal.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the setting. It was the next day (Luke 9:37), say Barnes and Ellicott, the night having been spent on the mount; and below, scribes were “questioning” the disciples (Mark 9:14), Barnes thinks “attempting to introduce a debate.” Ellicott warns that Raphael’s great painting, for all its power, has joined the glory above and the trouble below into one moment, though “the two scenes did not synchronise.” Clarke pictures the congregation gathered during the Lord’s absence on the mount and their patient waiting — “in the present day how rare is this zeal!” The Puritan Matthew Henry draws the comfort of the descent: “Christ’s glories do not make him unmindful of us and of our wants and miseries” — coming down from Moses and Elias, he “did not take state upon him, but was as easy of access” as ever.

The Latin fathers moralise the movement. Origen (here in their allegorical company) reads Peter, who had said “it is good for us to be here,” as “preferring his own benefit to that of many”; but Jesus, since “charity seeks not her own,” descended “as it were from the high mount of His divinity” to help those too weak to ascend. Jerome marks that the father, in saying “I brought him to thy disciples, and they could not heal him,” “covertly accuses the Apostles” — yet a cure may fail not from the healer’s want of power but the sufferer’s want of faith; and Christ’s coming rebuke is the firmness of a physician, not the temper of wrath.

Chrysostom notes the man appeals against the disciples before the multitude, but Christ “clears them from shame, imputing their failure to the patient himself,” who was weak in faith; Remigius hears in “How long shall I suffer you?” the Lord’s long endurance of Jewish stubbornness; and Raban and Augustine allegorise the boy’s fire and water as the heats of anger and the floods of fleshly lust.
15:
On Matthew 17:15 — “Lord, have mercy on my son: for he is lunatick, and sore vexed: for ofttimes he falleth into the fire, and oft into the water” — the witnesses describe the affliction and the father’s plea.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the symptoms and the word “lunatic.” Barnes notes that “Lord” here is but a title of civility, “Sir,” and gathers from the parallels that the boy was an only child, possessed, sore vexed (suffering greatly), and dumb “except when the fit was coming on him” (Matthew 4:24). Robertson and Clarke explain the Greek seleniazetai, “moonstruck,” since the symptoms were thought to wax and wane with the moon; Ellicott describes the phenomena, drawn from the other Gospels, as a “dumb spirit” that, when it seized the boy, “tore him,” so that he foamed and gnashed his teeth and was “wasting away under the violence of the attacks” — “epilepsy complicated with insanity, a combination common in all countries.” Lightfoot supplies the Talmudic learning on the deaf and the mad, who are reckoned with the child as unfit for sacred acts. Clarke marks the strategy of the case: “the devil intended to hide himself under the appearance of a natural disorder, that no supernatural means might be resorted to for his expulsion.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices keep both the natural and the spiritual together: Wesley allows “lunatic” to be used “with great propriety, though the case was mostly preternatural,” since the evil spirit “would undoubtedly take advantage of the influence which the changes of the moon have on the brain and nerves.”

The Reformed Gill turns to the father’s heart: he addresses Christ “with great marks of honour and respect,” and craves “mercy, pity, and compassion,” his case “a miserable one,” his affection the keener because, as Luke adds, this was his only child. The witnesses thus hold together a real bodily disorder and a real demonic oppression, and a father’s desperate, reverent appeal.

Barnes adds that the boy “fell often suddenly in the manner of persons having epileptic fits,” dumb save when the seizure came upon him — so that the father’s broken words paint a long and grievous bondage.
16:
On Matthew 17:16 — “And I brought him to thy disciples, and they could not cure him” — the witnesses, few here, ask why the disciples failed, and converge on a single answer.

The grammatical-historical commentators locate the failure. Barnes notes the boy was brought not to the apostles, “for they had power over unclean spirits” (Matthew 10:8), but “to others of his followers, who attempted to work miracles” — probably many disciples not personal attendants on his ministry (Mark 9:38). Clarke is blunter: “no wonder, when the cure must be effected by supernatural agency, and they had not faith enough to interest the power of God in their behalf” (v.20); “a spiritual disorder must have a spiritual remedy: natural means, in such cases, signify just — nothing.” Ellicott makes the failure the very matter of the scribes’ debate: the disciples “had probably trusted to their use of the wonted formula of their Master’s name,” and were now “wrangling in their own defence,” for “neither scribes nor disciples had thought of gaining the spiritual power which might avail.”

The Reformed Gill marks the father’s intent: “no doubt but his design was to bring him to Christ first; but he being absent” on the mount, he applied to the nine and “desired them to make use of their power to heal him; and which they attempted, but without success.”

The witnesses are few and of one mind: the disciples’ failure was no defect in Christ’s commission, nor in the formula they had been given, but in their own faith — a failure that lays bare the emptiness of a borrowed name pronounced without the living power that alone makes it effectual, and that sets up the rebuke and the lesson of the verses that follow.

The lesson the witnesses draw is uniform and sobering: a commission truly given may yet lie idle in unbelieving hands. The nine had the power; what they wanted was the faith to use it — and so their public failure, before the scribes and the crowd, becomes the occasion for the Lord’s teaching on the faith that removes mountains.
17:
On Matthew 17:17 — “O faithless and perverse generation, how long shall I suffer you?” — the witnesses divide chiefly over a single question: to whom is the rebuke addressed?

The grammatical-historical commentators define “perverse” and spread the blame. Robertson: diestrammene, “distorted, twisted in two, corrupt”; Barnes glosses it as “turned or perverted from the truth” — that they “hold opinions turned… from the truth” and are “wicked in their conduct” — and applies it “probably to the Jews, and not to his real disciples,” reading “how long shall I suffer you” as “how long shall I be with you.” Lightfoot judges the edge of the words “levelled especially against the scribes… and yet the disciples escaped not altogether untouched” — for the child could not be healed “partly because the devil was really in him, partly because this evil had adhered to him from his very birth,” whereupon the scribes “insult and scoff.” Ellicott reads it most evenly: the words were addressed “both to the scribes and the disciples,” for both lacked the faith “which utters itself in prayer,” and both were “perverse” in finding in the boy’s misery “only an occasion of wrangling.”

The Reformed voices press the rebuke onto the scribes. Calvin: though Christ seems to speak to the father, “there can be no doubt that he refers to the scribes” — men who “through inveterate malice obstinately resist God,” no longer worthy to be endured, whom he threatens ere long to leave; Gill agrees the phrase “is never used of the disciples,” and falls on the faithless scribes who triumphed over them.

Against this, the Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley holds that “our Lord speaks principally this to his disciples,” asking how long “before you steadfastly believe?”, while Clarke spreads the word over the disciples, the father (who “should have brought his son to Christ”), and the whole unbelieving multitude alike. The Alexandrian Origen lifts it higher, hearing in it the Saviour’s grief over “the whole race of men on earth,” oppressed by their wickedness.

Yet beneath the dividing of the address all the witnesses hear one note: the rebuke falls wherever faith is wanting, and bends, even in its severity, toward the healing that immediately follows.
18:
On Matthew 17:18 — “And Jesus rebuked the devil; and he departed out of him: and the child was cured from that very hour” — the witnesses mark the authority and completeness of the cure.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the word and fill in the scene from the parallels. Barnes: “rebuke” carries “the combined force of reproving and commanding” — he reproved the spirit for afflicting the child and commanded it to come out; Mark gives the very words, “Thou dumb and deaf spirit, I charge thee, come out of him, and enter no more into him” (Mark 9:25) — whereupon the spirit “cried, and with a mighty convulsion came out, leaving him apparently dead,” till Jesus “lifted him up by the hand” and gave him to his father. Ellicott prefers “demon” to “devil,” and notes the sufferer had “passed beyond the age of childhood” (Mark 9:21); after the great cry and convulsion many said, “He is dead,” but then came “calmness, and peace, and self-possession” — for “the spiritual power of the Healer had overcome the force, whether morbid or demoniac.” He adds that the New Testament “seems to recognise” in such disorders “an infraction of the divine order,” the work of “the great antagonist,” as Peter summed it: he “went about doing good, and healing all that were oppressed of the devil” (Acts 10:38). Clarke draws a pastoral note from Quesnel: “there are some souls whose cure God reserves to himself alone,” that ministers may know they can do no good without him.

The Reformed Gill is careful about the object of the rebuke: it was not the father, whom Christ had already reproved, nor the afflicted child, but “the foul,” or “unclean spirit,” as Mark and Luke expressly say — for though it was “a natural disease,” the boy was afflicted with it “in a preternatural way, by the means of Satan,” who “by divine permission… had a power of inflicting bodily diseases.” The witnesses agree in the upshot: at a word the unclean spirit, however fierce and long-seated, must go, and the boy is healed “from that very hour.”
19:
On Matthew 17:19 — “Then came the disciples to Jesus apart, and said, Why could not we cast him out?” — the witnesses gather around the answer of faith, which the Catena attaches richly here.

The Latin fathers are the most active. Jerome lays it down that “when we receive not, it is not the weakness of Him that gives, but the fault of them that ask” (Matthew 21:22); and he corrects those who read the mustard-seed faith as “little” faith — rather it is “great” and entire, since Paul speaks of a faith “that I could remove mountains” (1 Corinthians 13:2). Gregory adds that the mustard-seed, “unless it be bruised, does not give out its qualities,” so persecution rouses the holy man’s hidden virtue; Augustine warns the disciples, “by the humbleness of their faith,” to remove “all pride of earth, which is signified by the mountain.” Hilary notes that, while the Lord tarried on the mount and they abode below with the multitude, “their faith had become stagnant”; Raban teaches that “the heavier afflictions… may be removed by fasts and prayers,” and even “the wrath of the Lord” appeased; Remigius reads “fasting” broadly, as abstinence from all carnal allurements, and “prayer” as all pious acts (1 Thessalonians 5:17); the Gloss makes “this mountain” the proud devil, removed “into the sea, that is into the depths of hell.”

The Antiochene Chrysostom reads the disciples’ question as a fear that they had “forfeited the grace once given to them”: their faith “was grown weak, yet not all.” The Alexandrian Origen finds all faith likened to a mustard-seed because it “is looked on with contempt by men… but when a seed of this kind lights upon a good heart as its soil, it becomes a great tree.”

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene — the question was put privately, “in the house” (Mark) — and trace the failure to unbelief; Clarke turns it on the ministry: when our endeavours prove fruitless, we should “come, by private prayer, to Christ… and beg to be informed” whether some evil in ourselves be the cause. The Reformed Calvin closes the matter: the disciples “had lost it by their own fault,” for God “will never forsake us, if we keep the door open for receiving his grace.”
20:
On Matthew 17:20 — “Because of your unbelief… If ye have faith as a grain of mustard seed, ye shall say unto this mountain, Remove hence… and nothing shall be impossible unto you” — the witnesses weigh the measure and the meaning of this faith.

The grammatical-historical commentators note first the text. Ellicott and Robertson observe that many manuscripts read “little faith” (oligopistian) for “unbelief,” an example of “a tendency to tone down the apparent severity of our Lord’s words”; either way, they conclude, the disciples fell under the rebuke to the “faithless generation.” Barnes lays out two readings of the mustard-seed: the smallest of seeds may mean the feeblest genuine faith, or, since it grew to the largest of herbs (Matthew 13:31), an “increasing, expanding, enlarged faith.” The saying is hyperbolical (cf. Matthew 21:21); Robertson, following Bruce, even suspects “this mountain” pointed to the Mount of Transfiguration, “but it is a parable” — “our trouble is always with ‘this mountain’ which confronts our path.” Lightfoot notes the rabbinic idiom “a grain of mustard.”

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem rejoices in the figure: faith is “small in size, but fiery in its operation,” like the seed which “sown in a small space has a circle of great branches” and shelters the fowls; so faith “in the swiftest moment works the greatest effects in the soul,” which then “hath visions of God.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley adds a sober qualification: this faith “does not always imply saving faith,” for many have had it to cast out devils and yet “will at last have their portion with them” (1 Corinthians 13:2); it is “a supernatural persuasion… that God will work thus by him at that hour.”

The Reformed Gill takes “unbelief” as “the smallness of their faith,” charged to the disciples and not to the father; and the Alexandrian Origen reads the mountain as the boy’s disorder, cast out only by “the whole faith” of the healer. The witnesses agree on the heart of it: the least true faith, vigorous toward God, is mightier than the greatest obstacle.
21:
On Matthew 17:21 — “Howbeit this kind goeth not out but by prayer and fasting” — the witnesses dispute both the reading of the verse and the reference of “this kind.”

The grammatical-historical commentators take “this kind” of the demons. Barnes: it means “this kind of devils; this species of possession,” where they have had “long possession” and produce “painful, and fixed, and alarming effects,” expelled “only in connexion with prayer and fasting” — for the highest faith “is produced and kept vigorous only by” them. Ellicott finds the words imply “degrees in the intensity of the forms of evil… amounting to a generic difference”: some “might yield before the energy of a human will, and the power of the divine Name, and the prayers even of a weak faith,” while some, like this, “required a greater intensity of the spiritual life.” Clarke notes that some apply “this kind” to the faith that “goeth not out” (is not exerted) but by prayer and fasting, but judges that reading “far from solid”; and he adds the textual difficulty — “the whole verse is wanting in the famous Vatican MS.… and in another one of Colbert’s.” Lightfoot cites the rabbinic parallel that “by reason of an evil spirit a religious man may afflict himself with fastings.”

The Reformed Calvin reads “this kind of devils” as a reproof of negligence: “it is not every kind… that will suffice, when we have to enter into a serious conflict with Satan, but… vigorous efforts are indispensably necessary” — otherwise the disciples might have pleaded that they were “not altogether destitute” of faith. Gill agrees the words mark “a fierce and obstinate kind” of devil, hard to eject after long possession.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley seizes the practical point: “What a testimony is here of the efficacy of fasting, when added to fervent prayer!” — for “some kinds of devils the apostles had cast out before this, without fasting.” Thus, whatever the textual doubt, the witnesses agree on the lesson: the fiercest spiritual conflicts demand a faith braced by prayer and self-denial.

Gill adds that this was “a fierce and obstinate kind” that, “having had long possession, was not easily ejected” — so that the saying measures not Christ’s power but the discipline our own faith requires.
22:
On Matthew 17:22 — “The Son of man shall be betrayed into the hands of men” — the witnesses read this renewed passion-prediction, and weigh its words.

The Latin fathers find purpose in the repetition. Remigius: the Lord often foretold “the mysteries of His passion, in order that when they come to pass, they might be the lighter to them from having been known beforehand.” Jerome marks how Christ “ever mix[es] the joyful and the grievous” — if his death grieves them, his rising “the third day” should gladden them; and their exceeding sorrow “came not of their lack of faith; but out of their love of their Master.” Chrysostom notes “this is no long time that He speaks of continuing in death.” The Alexandrian Origen presses the new word: not before had Christ said he would be “betrayed into the hands of men” — the Son was “delivered up” by the Father (Romans 8:32), yet “different powers gave him up into the hands of men.”

The grammatical-historical commentators turn on that same verb. Barnes takes “betray” of Judas, who delivered him “in a treacherous manner” (Matthew 26:14); Clarke, “fully of the mind of” Grotius and Wakefield, prefers “delivered up,” referring the agency to God — “Jesus was delivered up by the counsel of God.” Ellicott reads “as they went to and fro” in Galilee, the Lord taking the disciples apart for “fuller disclosures of His coming passion,” willing “that no man should know” of their presence (Mark 9:30).

The Reformed Calvin gives the pastoral aim: “the nearer that the time of his death approached, the more frequently did Christ warn his disciples, lest that melancholy spectacle might give a violent shock to their faith”; the Puritan Henry marvels that, foreknowing all, he yet “undertook the work of our redemption, which greatly commends his love”; and the Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem insists he “came to His passion willingly,” foreknowing it and setting his face to Jerusalem, “lest the whole world should perish in its sins.”

So the witnesses hold the prediction together: a true betrayal by men, yet within the Father’s saving purpose, and never without the answering word of hope — “the third day.”
23:
On Matthew 17:23 — “And they shall kill him, and the third day he shall be raised again. And they were exceeding sorry” — the witnesses dwell on the promise of resurrection and the disciples’ grief.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the two halves together. Barnes joins the rising “the third day” (Matthew 12:40) to the sorrow, and lays out three reasons for it from the parallels: first, they were “strongly attached to him, and were exceedingly sorry… at any intimation that” he was soon to leave them, and so “learned with great slowness and reluctance”; second, “they were not willing to believe it,” for they supposed the Messiah was to be “a distinguished Prince” who would “restore the kingdom to Israel” (Acts 1:6), and a betrayal and death “appeared… to be frustrating all these expectations”; third, though the words were plain, “they did not understand… till after the resurrection.” Robertson reads the sorrow as the sign that “they at last understood that he was talking about his death and resurrection”; Ellicott, citing Mark and Luke that “it was hid from them… and they were afraid to ask Him” (Mark 9:32; Luke 9:45), calls their sorrow “vague and dim,” from which “they shrank… lest it should be made more definite”; and Clarke notes that the three, since the conversation on the mount, “could have no doubt that their Lord and Master must suffer,” and that “it was for this end he came into the world,” yet their affection caused “exquisite distress.”

The Reformed Gill marks that Christ told them plainly, while still in Galilee, that he should be put to death “with the death of the cross.” The witnesses thus hold the verse’s two notes together: a clear promise that death would not be the end — the third day a rising — met by a love so set on their Master that for now it could hear only the grief, and not yet the hope.

So the witnesses set grief and gospel side by side: the disciples could feel only the loss, while the promise that would one day be their whole comfort — the third-day rising — lay as yet “hid from them.”
24:
On Matthew 17:24 — “they that received tribute money came to Peter, and said, Doth not your master pay tribute?” — the witnesses agree on the nature of this payment and read its occasion.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the “tribute” precisely as the didrachma, the half-shekel Temple-rate required of every adult male Israelite (Exodus 30:13; 2 Chronicles 24:9), and not the Roman poll-tax. Robertson, with Plummer, stresses that it was “like a voluntary church-rate; no one could be compelled to pay”; Lightfoot cites Josephus, that the tax of “two drachms” was brought yearly, as “before they had paid it into the Temple at Jerusalem”; Barnes and Clarke agree it went to the temple-service by the law of Moses. The Puritan Henry: “not any civil payment to the Roman powers… but the church-duties, the half shekel,” which Christ “came to Capernaum” ready to pay.

The Latin fathers read it typologically and morally. Chrysostom traces the tax to the redemption-money for the firstborn: when God slew Egypt’s firstborn he “accepted the tribe of Levi for them” (Numbers 3:44), and, the Levites falling short of the firstborn’s number, “redemption money was paid for the number that came short” — so the collectors came to Peter, since Christ was a firstborn son and Peter “first among the disciples.” Hilary makes the coin in the fish’s mouth a figure of Stephen, the “first martyr,” who “preached the didrachma of the new preaching”; Augustine reasons that “in every kingdom the children are free,” and much more those who are “children of that very kingdom under which are all the kingdoms of the earth”; Gregory draws the rule of charity — we must, “as far as we may without sin,… avoid giving scandal,” though “if offence is taken from truth, it is better that offence should come.”

The Reformed Calvin gathers the design: Christ, by paying of his own accord, “declared his subjection” (Philippians 2:7), yet showed by word and miracle that it was “a free and voluntary submission,” not of necessity; and they came to Peter, he conjectures, “because Christ lived with him.” The Alexandrian Origen turns the saying allegorical — the truly free are “sons of God” though strangers to the kings of the earth.
25:
On Matthew 17:25 — “He saith, Yes. And when he was come into the house, Jesus prevented him, saying… of whom do the kings of the earth take custom or tribute? of their own children, or of strangers?” — the witnesses read Peter’s ready answer and Christ’s anticipating question.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the old word “prevented.” Barnes: Jesus “spoke before Peter” had told him what had passed — “prevent here means to go before, or precede,” not, as now, to hinder (Psalm 59:10; 1 Thessalonians 4:15); the verse thus quietly shows Christ’s knowledge of what Peter had said in his absence. Robertson notes the rare verb (proephthasen), found here only in the New Testament and in a papyrus of B.C. 161, meaning “to anticipate, to get before one in doing a thing,” and that “the ‘prevent’ of the Authorized Version” is archaic. Ellicott observes Peter’s answer was “ready enough” — his Master would pay as he had paid before, as every devout Israelite paid — given “without thought of the altered conditions of the case,” and notes the application to Peter suggests Christ was “domiciled in the house of Peter.”

The Reformed Calvin calls Peter’s “Yes” “a very modest and civil excuse” — “he will pay” — from which “we infer that Christ had formerly been accustomed to pay”; and again that they addressed Peter “because Christ lived with him.” The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the civic lesson: Christ “customarily paid all taxes”; the children of God are “subject to all civil laws… and should pay the taxes levied on them,” for their business “is not to reform the politics of nations, but the morals of the world.”

The Alexandrian Origen turns the kings’ sons into “sons of God,” “free” because they have “known the truth”; and the Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem hears beneath the figure the deeper sonship — the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, “Thou art My Son, this day have I begotten thee” (Psalm 2:7). The plain sense, on which all rest, is that the Son owes no tribute to his own Father’s house.
26:
On Matthew 17:26 — “Peter saith unto him, Of strangers. Jesus saith unto him, Then are the children free” — the witnesses draw out the argument: the Son owes nothing to his own Father’s house.

The grammatical-historical commentators complete the logic. Robertson: “the sons” are Christ — and the disciples too, “in contrast with the Jews”; thus, in reply to Peter’s prompt “Yes,” they are “logically… free from the temple tax, but practically not,” as he goes on to show. Ellicott sets the saying against “the Eastern rather than the European theory of taxation,” in which direct taxation was “hateful as a sign of subjugation” — it had roused revolt under Rehoboam (1 Kings 12:4), where they “stoned the officer who was over the tribute,” groaning under it again under the Syrian kings and under Herod, and Judas of Galilee had headed an insurrection against the Roman tax (Acts 5:37); whether a Jew might lawfully pay it was “still… a moot point” (Matthew 22:17). He presses the words past Peter’s mere confession to a wider truth, for to read the Israelites who paid as “strangers” would set them “on the level of a ‘stranger,’ not of a ‘son’”; the true law for “the children of the kingdom” is Paul’s, “not grudgingly, or of necessity: for God loveth a cheerful giver” (2 Corinthians 9:7).

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices make the sonship explicit. Clarke: “as this money is levied for the support of that temple of which I am the Lord, then I am not obliged to pay the tax”; and his disciples, “like the priests that minister, should be exempted.” Wesley puts the whole syllogism: “This is paid for the use of the house of God. But I am the Son of God. Therefore I am free from any obligation of paying this to my own Father.”

The Reformed Gill simply records that the words are Peter’s answer. The witnesses agree: by right the Son is exempt — which makes the payment that immediately follows wholly an act of free condescension.

Gill simply records the words as Peter’s, the hinge on which the Lord turns the lesson toward the freedom of the sons.
27:
On Matthew 17:27 — “Notwithstanding, lest we should offend them, go thou to the sea… thou shalt find a piece of money: that take, and give unto them for me and thee” — the witnesses read together the lesson of charity and the proof of dominion.

The grammatical-historical commentators handle the act and the coin. Barnes: though not obliged, “yet it is best to pay it,” lest they seem to despise the temple and its service and so “provoke needless opposition”; the “piece of money” is a stater, a silver coin “of the value of four drachms, or one shekel,” and so “sufficient to pay the” tribute for two. Clarke and Wesley agree on the exact fitness of the sum — four drachms, two shekels, “just the sum that was wanted.” Robertson notes the verb “lest we cause them to stumble” (skandalisomen) and that this is “the only example in the N.T. of fishing with a hook” (agkistron). Ellicott catches a tone of “half-playful, half-serious irony,” recalling that the last time at Capernaum the disciples had remonstrated when Christ’s teaching offended the Pharisees (Matthew 15:12); now he himself yields, where no principle forbids.

The Reformed Calvin reads the miracle as the counterweight to the condescension: not poverty but design moved Christ to send Peter to the sea, “to prove by a miracle that he had a more extensive dominion than all earthly kings,” having even the fish “for his tributaries” — and “we do not read that this was done more than once.” Gill draws the pastoral rule: Christ “chose to forego his right… lest any should be offended with him,” which “may teach us to be careful to give no offence, to Jew or Gentile.”

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley turns it to peace: yield to an unjust demand “rather than violate peace or love… Any thing which is not expressly forbidden in the word of God”; and he hears the tender “for me and thee,” Christ providing for Peter as for himself. So the witnesses unite the chapter’s close: free, yet for love’s sake paying; Lord of the sea, yet stooping to a single stater.
